cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Welcome in, good to have you here today.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Let's run a quick Playwright round, the kind of rapid technical screen you'd get for an SDET role.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: I'll ask a question, you give me your best answer, and I may push a little deeper here and there.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Nothing exotic, just the fundamentals done well.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Ready when you are.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: Sounds good, let's get into it.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Let's start at the top.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: You've used Selenium before, so tell me, what actually makes Playwright different, beyond it just being newer?

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The core difference is the protocol and what's built in.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: Playwright talks to the browser over a persistent DevTools style connection instead of the WebDriver wire protocol, so it's one process holding an open channel to Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit through a single API.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: Why that matters is speed and reliability, there's no request and response handshake per command, and Playwright layers auto-waiting into every single action, so the flaky wait logic you'd assemble yourself in Selenium comes for free.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: On top of that you get network interception, tracing, video, and screenshots without bolting on third party tools.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: A concrete example, in a Selenium suite I'd maintain a custom explicit wait helper for every dynamic element, and in Playwright that whole helper class disappears because a click already waits for the element to be visible, stable, and enabled.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The trap is assuming they're interchangeable, the architecture is genuinely different.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Good framing.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: You mentioned the browser once, so let's go there, walk me through the browser, context, and page model.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: Three layers.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The browser is the actual instance, it's expensive to launch, so you reuse one across the whole run.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: A browser context is an isolated session inside that browser, its own cookies, storage, and cache, essentially a fresh incognito profile, and it's cheap to create.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: A page is a single tab inside a context, and that's where most interactions happen.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The why is the trade-off between speed and isolation, relaunching a browser per test would be clean but painfully slow, while sharing one session would leak state between tests.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: Contexts give you both, one browser, but every test gets its own clean context, so no test sees another test's cookies or login.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: In practice that means a test that logs in as an admin and a test that checks the logged out view can run back to back, or even in parallel, without ever polluting each other, and that context isolation is exactly what makes safe parallel execution possible.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Nice, and you flagged the interview point yourself, that isolation without relaunching is the key.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Next one, how do you choose locators, and what is auto-waiting actually doing for you?

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: My first choice is getByRole, locating by the ARIA role and accessible name, because it matches how a user and assistive technology perceive the page, so it survives markup refactors that would kill a CSS class or an id selector.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: After that I reach for getByLabel, getByText, or a stable data-testid hook, and CSS or XPath only as a fallback.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The deeper point is that a locator is lazy, it re-queries the DOM every time you act on it, which is why stale element problems basically vanish in Playwright.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: Auto-waiting means that before any action fires, Playwright waits for the element to be attached, visible, stable, enabled, and able to receive events, and web-first assertions like expecting a locator to be visible keep retrying until the condition holds or times out.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The trap I call out in reviews is someone grabbing the text content once and asserting on that snapshot, that races on slow machines, whereas the retrying assertion just waits it out.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: That distinction between a one-shot snapshot and a retrying assertion trips up a lot of people, well spotted.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Now, logging in through the UI before every test gets expensive fast.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: How do you handle authentication across a big suite?

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The pattern is authenticate once and reuse the state.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: In a global setup step I log in through the app one time, then save the storage state, which is the cookies plus local storage, out to a JSON file.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The config then points new contexts at that file, so every test starts with the session already active and never touches the login screen.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The why is both speed and stability, login is usually the slowest and one of the flakiest flows in any app, so removing it from hundreds of tests is a massive win, it's the equivalent of seeding a logged in session instead of clicking through the form each time.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: On one suite that alone shaved minutes off every CI run.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: It also extends naturally to multiple roles, you save one state file per role, admin, editor, viewer, and a test that needs two roles just opens two contexts, each loading its own file.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The trap is doing both, loading saved state and also running a login fixture, which double authenticates and can create session conflicts.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Solid, that's the pattern I wanted to hear.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Let's talk about the network.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Your UI test depends on a backend that's slow or flaky, what do you do?

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: I isolate the UI from the backend with network interception.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: Playwright lets me register a route against a URL pattern, and inside that handler I can fulfill the request with fixed JSON of my own, modify it, or abort it entirely to simulate a network failure.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: So for a deterministic UI test I stub the API, the users endpoint returns exactly the payload I define, every run, and now the test is verifying my rendering logic rather than the backend's mood that day.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The why is determinism, a UI test should fail because the UI broke, not because staging was slow.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: A concrete case, testing an empty state screen used to mean somehow arranging an account with no data, instead I intercept the list endpoint and fulfill it with an empty array, and the empty state renders instantly.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: When I do need the real backend, I can wait for a specific response before asserting.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The trap is over-mocking, if everything is stubbed you're no longer testing integration, so I keep a thin layer of end to end tests against the real API alongside the mocked ones.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Good balance at the end there, mocking is a tool, not a religion.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Last one, and it's the practical one.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: A test passes locally every time but fails intermittently in CI.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Walk me through how you attack that.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: My first stop is the trace, not re-running the test.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: I configure tracing to record on the first retry, so when CI retries a failed test, Playwright captures a full trace, DOM snapshots before and after every step, the network waterfall, and console output.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: I open that in the Trace Viewer and step through the failure frame by frame, which usually shows me a timing issue, an element momentarily detached during a re-render, or an API call that takes ten times longer on the CI box than on my machine.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The why is that CI flakiness is almost always a race the faster local environment hides, so I need evidence from the failing environment, not a local reproduction that will never fail.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: A real example, a continue button that was briefly disabled while a validation call finished, locally the call was instant, in CI it wasn't, and the fix was asserting the button was enabled before clicking, letting auto-waiting absorb the delay.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The trap is just bumping the timeout, that hides the race instead of fixing it, and retries should be a safety net, not the cure.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Strong answers, that's the round.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: What I liked here, and what listeners should take away, is the structure, a direct answer first, then the why, and then a concrete example or a trap called out.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: That pattern is what makes an answer sound senior.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Practice these until they flow, and I'll see you in the next round.
